story:
A man wakes tied naked to a chair, a tarp spread beneath him. He roars and thrashes until I step into his view. "Edward Miller," I say. "I'm Stella White. You don't know me, but I know you." He curses, demands I let him go. "Do you know why you're here?" I ask. He just spits insults. I circle him, pull an eight-inch knife from my boot, and lay the blade along his shoulder, a warning. I ignore them. Pain sharpens his attention. I take my time. When blood runs and his muscles jerk, the sight drags me somewhere I don't want to go.

I was eight when my mother killed my father. Blood everywhere—floor, walls, curtains. She stood over his body, knife in her hand, eyes emptied out. I tried to run. I couldn't. Later, they took her away. I landed in foster care. I promised myself I would never become her.

Years passed before I went to see her. We sat with thick glass between us, a phone on each side. Her hair was wild, her eyes hollow. I asked one word: "Why?" She said she did not have a choice. I exploded—there are always choices. She begged me to listen. That night, she told me, my father raped her three times. Then he said he was done with her, and he was coming for me. "I could not let him," she whispered. "I chose prison over letting him touch you." My world twisted. The father I defended became a stranger. The mother I hated had been a shield. She stared past me as if trapped inside a memory she couldn't escape.

Back in the present, Edward screams through the pain in his arm, still not answering my question. "No idea at all?" I ask. He just bellows. So I tell him. Forty-seven girls. None older than twelve. He stole their childhoods, their sense of safety, their ownership of their own bodies. He turned their skin into a place of fear. There's no way to make him feel exactly what he made them feel, but I can make him face a fraction of the harm he caused. He looks at me, pale and sweating, and finally understands that this is not a game. The finale is not for show.

I move the blade. He freezes. I do what I came to do, clean and fast. His face collapses with the shock. I don't gloat. I don't celebrate. I tell him he will not walk away with the tools he used to break children. I force him to confront the consequences—immediate, undeniable. He shakes his head, gagging, eyes wide with animal panic. I don't look away. I don't pretend this is justice in any official sense. It isn't. It's what happens when protection is too late and accountability fails until someone decides failing is no longer acceptable.

He chokes on terror, on what he's done, on what remains of who he was. I sit in front of him, steady and close, and wait for him to feel the weight of it all—the blood, the harm, the truth. I am my mother's daughter, whether I wanted to be or not. And tonight, I made sure another child will not become me.
